title: Star Wars (1977) 2

{{Quote|Swing that lightsabre, Ben—or we're finished!|Luke Skywalker, to Obi-Wan Kenobi|<ref name="Star Wars (1977) 2">[[Star Wars (1977) 2|''Star Wars'' (1977) 2]]</ref>}}
'''''Star Wars 2: Six Against the Galaxy''''' is the second issue of [[Star Wars (1977)|Marvel's ''Star Wars'']] series of comics, and part two of the six-part adaptation of [[Star Wars: Episode IV A New Hope|''A New Hope'']].

==Plot summary==
[[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker]] is rescued from an attacking band of [[Tusken Raider/Legends|Sand People]] by [[Obi-Wan Kenobi/Legends|Obi-Wan Kenobi]]. Once Luke, Obi-Wan, and the two [[Droid/Legends|droids]] [[R2-D2/Legends|R2-D2]] and [[C-3PO/Legends|C-3PO]] are safely in [[Ben Kenobi's home/Legends|Obi-Wan Kenobi's hut]], R2 plays a recorded message for Kenobi from the [[Princess/Legends|Princess]] [[Leia Organa Solo/Legends|Leia Organa]] asking [[Gender/Legends|him]] to bring the little droid and the critical information contained within its memory to [[Alderaan/Legends|Alderaan]]. Initially Luke refuses to accompany Obi-Wan on his journey because of his obligations at home, but upon learning that his home has been destroyed and his uncle [[Owen Lars/Legends|Owen]] and aunt [[Beru Whitesun Lars/Legends|Beru]] have been [[Death|killed]] by [[Galactic Empire/Legends|Imperial]] [[Stormtrooper/Legends|stormtroopers]], he agrees to go along. They hire [[Han Solo/Legends|Han Solo]] and [[Chewbacca/Legends|Chewbacca]] to [[Pilot/Legends|pilot]] them to Alderaan aboard the [[Millennium Falcon/Legends|''Millennium Falcon'']].

==Continuity==
This is the first comic-book appearance for Obi-Wan Kenobi, Han Solo, and Chewbacca. The [[Lightsaber/Legends|lightsaber]] makes its comic debut in this issue, although it is spelled "lightsabre," and has a pink blade rather than blue.

The cover art is misleading as at no point in the cantina brawl, in the film or its comics adaptation, does Luke fire a blaster or exhort Ben to fight.

This issue includes the Han Solo and [[Jabba Desilijic Tiure/Legends|Jabba the Hutt]] [[Docking Bay 94/Legends|Docking Bay 94]] scene that wasn't finished for the film. ([[Hutt/Legends|Hutt]] is spelled "Hut" in this issue, as it was in all official ''Star Wars'' material before [[1983]].) Rather than portray Jabba as he appeared in the filmed version of the scene (a large Human wearing furs, as played by [[Declan Mulholland]])—which was never meant to be how Jabba would have looked in the film anyway, had the scene been completed—the likeness of [[Mosep Binneed/Legends|Mosep Binneed]], a [[Nimbanel/Legends|Nimbanel]] seen in the background during the Mos Eisley scenes, was used. This likeness of Jabba was also used in [[Star Wars (1977) 28|''Star Wars'' (1977) 28]] and [[Star Wars (1977) 37|''Star Wars'' (1977) 37]]. After [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Return of the Jedi'']] officially established what Jabba looked like, it was decided to make Mosep an associate of Jabba, and the Nimbanese a species of Hutt servants. The article "[[Red Five (department)|Red Five]]" in [[Star Wars Insider 149|''Star Wars Insider'' 149]] has established that the "Jabba" in the Marvel comics is in fact Mosep, who sometimes used Jabba's name in interactions.

Similarly, [[Garindan/Legends|Garindan]] and the [[IT-O interrogation droid/Legends|IT-O interrogation droid]] look different in this issue from the way they do in the film. As editor [[Roy Thomas]] would explain in the letters column of [[Star Wars (1977) 7|issue 7]], much of the artwork for the early Marvel issues was based on an unfinished cut of the film with incomplete post-production, so the Marvel artists didn't have access to many final images before their version went to press.

In her message to Obi-Wan Kenobi, Princess Leia states that one "Bail Antillies" is her father. [[Bail Antilles/Legends|Bail Antilles]] was later mentioned in [[Star Wars: Episode I The Phantom Menace|''The Phantom Menace'']]. In [[2007]], the Marvel adaptation of ''A New Hope'' was released in a trade paperback and bundled with the [[Original unaltered trilogy|2006 DVD]] release of ''A New Hope''. "Bail Antillies" was replaced with "[[Bail Prestor Organa/Legends|Bail Organa]]" in this edition.

When Luke discovers his home destroyed along with his aunt and uncle, he calls for his Uncle Ben instead of Uncle Owen. This was most likely an error.

On the cover of both this issue and the previous one, the first and last two letters of the ''Star Wars'' logo are not attached to each other.

==Media==
{{Mediacat|from=1|imagecat=Images from Star Wars (1977) 2}}
The original cover price for this issue was 30 cents. There were also several reprint versions, easily identified by "Reprint" in the upper left corner of the cover or on the inside indicia, or by the price and number appearing inside a diamond on the cover with no date or UPC. These "Reprint" versions were found in the bagged comic three packs commonly found at department and drug stores in the 1970s and 1980s. The first eighteen or so issues of this series are available in the bagged three packs.

In [[1994]], [[Dark Horse Comics]] acquired the rights to reprint this issue and the other parts in the adaptation.

In [[2007]], this issue was reprinted and included in [[Hasbro]]'s Comic Packs along with a [[Governor/Legends|Governor]] [[Wilhuff Tarkin/Legends|Tarkin]] and a stormtrooper action figure.

Along with the first issue, this is one of the most reprinted comics of all time. It has been reprinted along with the other five parts of the adaptation in standard formats and also large treasury size and small novel size editions.
